title: Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
summary: Aggregates, entities, value objects, domain events, bounded contexts, and applying DDD in Spring Boot microservices.

sections.content:
## What is DDD?

Domain-Driven Design (Eric Evans, 2003) is an approach to software development that puts the **business domain at the center** of design. The software model mirrors the domain expert's mental model, using the same language (Ubiquitous Language).

### Strategic vs Tactical DDD
- **Strategic** — large-scale structure: Bounded Contexts, Context Maps, Ubiquitous Language
- **Tactical** — fine-grained patterns: Aggregates, Entities, Value Objects, Domain Events, Repositories

sections.title: DDD Building Blocks
sections.content:
graph TD
    BC[Bounded Context] --> Agg[Aggregate]
    Agg --> E[Entity\nHas identity\nmutable]
    Agg --> VO[Value Object\nNo identity\nimmutable]
    Agg --> DE[Domain Events]
    BC --> Repo[Repository\nPersistence abstraction]
    BC --> DS[Domain Service\nStateless domain logic]
    BC --> AS[Application Service\nOrchestration layer]
    style BC fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Agg fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.title: Aggregate Boundaries
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Order Aggregate
        OR[Order Root\nOrderId: 123] --> OL1[OrderLine\nProduct A]
        OR --> OL2[OrderLine\nProduct B]
        OR --> Addr[ShippingAddress\nValue Object]
    end
    subgraph Customer Aggregate
        CR[Customer Root\nCustomerId: 456]
    end
    OR -->|reference by ID only| CR
    Note[External code accesses\nOrderLine through Order only]
    style OR fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style CR fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Bounded Contexts

A Bounded Context defines the boundary within which a particular **Ubiquitous Language** is consistent. The same word can mean different things in different contexts.

```
"Order" in Order Context: line items, status, payment
"Order" in Shipping Context: destination, weight, dimensions
"Order" in Analytics Context: revenue, category, timestamp

Don't try to build one Order model that serves all contexts!
Each context has its own model.
```

### Context Map patterns:
- **Shared Kernel** — two contexts share a small subset of models
- **Customer/Supplier** — upstream provides API for downstream
- **Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL)** — translate between different models at boundary
- **Published Language** — well-documented shared protocol (like Protobuf schema)

sections.question: Why should only the Aggregate Root be accessible from outside an aggregate?
options: For performance reasons — accessing child entities is slower
To enforce all business invariants through a single entry point, preventing the aggregate from entering an invalid state
Because child entities don't have IDs and can't be referenced
sections.explanation: The Aggregate Root is the guardian of the aggregate's invariants (business rules). All modifications must go through it so it can enforce rules like 'order lines can only be added to draft orders.' If external code could directly modify OrderLine, it could bypass these rules and put the aggregate in an invalid state.
